summary: ValleyDAO blog post about Cocoa Zero project at Imperial College London, developing microbial production of cocoa fats. Led by Dr. Oliver Konzock and Dr. Rodrigo Ledesma-Amaro. Engineered yeast strains achieved fatty acid profile matching cocoa butter. Project funded February 2024, now moving to chocolate manufacturer testing and commercialization.

content_blocks.title: Project Overview
content_blocks.content: In February 2024, ValleyDAO funded research initiative at Imperial College London led by Dr. Oliver Konzock under supervision of Dr. Rodrigo Ledesma-Amaro to develop method for producing cocoa fats using microbes similar to beer brewing yeast. Goal: create sustainable, ethical, reliable alternative to traditional cocoa facing climate challenges and rising prices.

content_blocks.title: Climate and Market Context
content_blocks.content: Cocoa farming heavily impacted by climate change with fluctuating weather patterns disrupting supply chains. Cocoa prices surged nearly 300% since 2023. Microbial production offers more stable, reliable, sustainable source while reducing environmental impact.

content_blocks.title: Cocoa Price Chart
content_blocks.content: Graph showing cocoa price ($/MTon) from approximately 1998-2025. Price remained relatively stable around $2,500-3,000 from 1998-2022, then spiked dramatically starting 2023 to peak over $12,000/MTon by 2025.

content_blocks.title: Technical Milestone Achievement
content_blocks.content: Team at Imperial achieved significant milestone: engineered yeast strains produce fats with fatty acid profile closely resembling real cocoa butter. Critical achievement enables mimicking melting point, mouthfeel, and texture of real chocolate essential for consumer acceptance.

content_blocks.title: Fatty Acid Profile Comparison
content_blocks.content: Radar chart comparing fatty acid profiles across five components: C16.0 (palmitic), C16.1, C18.0 (stearic), C18.1 (oleic), C18.2 (linoleic). Three profiles shown: Cocoa Butter (dark green/black), ValleyDAO Engineered Strain (light green), Non-Engineered Strain (orange/tan). ValleyDAO strain closely matches cocoa butter profile across all five fatty acids, while non-engineered strain shows significant deviations.

content_blocks.title: Fatty Acid Functions
content_blocks.content: Precise tuning of yeast metabolism produces key fatty acids: palmitic (C16:0), stearic (C18:0), oleic (C18:1), linoleic (C18:2). Functions: palmitic and stearic acids provide firmness and texture; oleic acid adds smoothness and pleasant melting sensation; linoleic acid influences softness and flexibility.

content_blocks.title: Team Background
content_blocks.content: Dr. Oliver Konzock: expertise in cocoa fat bioproduction from Chalmers University and UC Berkeley research position, developed innovative microbial methods for cocoa butter alternatives. Dr. Rodrigo Ledesma-Amaro: expert in metabolic engineering and synthetic biology, recently appointed Director of Imperial's Bezos Centre for Sustainable Protein, reflects leadership in novel foods innovation.

content_blocks.title: Next Steps and Commercialization
content_blocks.content: ValleyDAO partnering with chocolate manufacturers to test bioproduced fats in real chocolate formulations. Next step: scaling process and moving toward commercialization. If successful, could lead to first DeSci-enabled chocolate bar, marking major step bringing DeSci innovations into consumer products.

content_blocks.title: ValleyDAO Funding Model
content_blocks.content: DeSci collective funding climate-focused biotech research. $GROW token holders participate in research crowdfunding, governance decisions, and share in impact. Community stake in scientific impact through tokenization.

key_findings: February 2024: ValleyDAO funded Imperial College London project for microbial cocoa fat production
Engineered yeast strains achieved fatty acid profile closely matching real cocoa butter across C16:0, C16:1, C18:0, C18:1, C18:2
Technology mimics melting point, mouthfeel, texture of real chocolate

Led by Dr. Oliver Konzock (cocoa fat bioproduction expertise from Chalmers/UC Berkeley) and Dr. Rodrigo Ledesma-Amaro (metabolic engineering, Director of Imperial's Bezos Centre for Sustainable Protein)
Currently partnering with chocolate manufacturers for testing in real formulations
Next phase: scaling process toward commercialization of first DeSci-enabled chocolate bar
Market driver: cocoa prices increased 300% since 2023 due to climate impacts on farming
